title: OPEC Production Collapses 30% as Hormuz Crisis Enters Third Month
subhead: Oil markets have lost over 1 billion barrels of supply. Canada moves to build new west coast pipeline. US-Brazil ethanol exports surge as consumers seek alternatives.

sections.heading: OPEC Production Plunges as Hormuz Closure Tightens
sections.body:
OPEC oil production has fallen by more than 30% since the Iran war began in late February, with output dropping 9.7 million barrels per day across member states, according to the cartel's latest monthly report. Gulf producers have borne the brunt: Saudi Arabia cut production by 3.3 million bpd (33%), Kuwait by 1.98 million bpd (76%), Iraq by 2.8 million bpd (66%), and the UAE by 1.4 million bpd (40%).

The International Energy Agency estimates total supply losses now exceed 1 billion barrels, with more than 14 million bpd shut in due to the Hormuz closure. Global oil inventories depleted by 250 million barrels in March and April alone — a record drawdown rate of 4 million bpd.

Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned that markets may not normalize until 2027 if the disruption continues, calling it "the largest energy supply shock the world has ever experienced." The world is currently losing about 100 million barrels per week while the strait remains largely closed to tanker traffic.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale of supply disruption is unprecedented. The drawdown of strategic reserves is buying time but cannot offset a sustained closure. The longer the crisis persists, the more structural the market damage becomes.

sections.heading: UAE Accelerates Pipeline Expansion to Bypass Hormuz
sections.body:
The UAE announced plans to accelerate construction of a new oil pipeline that would double its export capacity bypassing the Strait of Hormuz by 2027. The project would significantly expand exports through Fujairah on the Gulf of Oman, reducing reliance on the blocked chokepoint.

Saudi Arabia's East-West pipeline has already reached its maximum capacity of 7 million barrels per day, with 2 million bpd supplying domestic refineries and 5 million bpd available for export via Yanbu on the Red Sea. Aramco is now considering expanding export capacity at Yanbu.

Iraq, which lacks comparable bypass infrastructure, has seen exports through Hormuz collapse from about 93 million barrels monthly to just 10 million barrels in April — a 90% drop, according to the country's new oil minister.
sections.whyItMatters: Gulf states with pipeline infrastructure are weathering the crisis better than those without. The acceleration of bypass projects reflects a permanent shift in risk perception — Hormuz is no longer viewed as reliably open.

sections.heading: US-Brazil Ethanol Exports Surge as Fuel Alternatives Gain Demand
sections.body:
The world's two largest ethanol producers are seeing export increases as consuming countries seek alternative fuel sources during the Hormuz crisis. The United States and Brazil are both ramping up biofuel exports to meet demand from nations looking to diversify fuel supplies away from Middle Eastern crude.

Rising energy security concerns are driving expanding ethanol blending programs across Asia, supporting corn and sugar markets while both countries increase biofuel production capacity.
sections.whyItMatters: The crisis is accelerating the diversification of global fuel supply chains. Biofuels are emerging as a strategic hedge against maritime chokepoint disruptions.

sections.heading: Canada Signs Deal for New West Coast Oil Pipeline
sections.body:
Prime Minister Mark Carney and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith signed a landmark climate and energy agreement that could see construction on a new oil pipeline to the Pacific Coast begin as early as September 2027. The deal includes Alberta submitting a pipeline proposal by July 1, with federal designation as a project of national interest by October 1.

The pipeline would carry oil no later than 2033-2034, providing Canadian crude access to Asian markets independent of US infrastructure. Alberta will act as project proponent, with the federal government committing to streamline regulatory approvals.

The agreement also raises Alberta's industrial carbon price to $130/tonne by 2040, though at a slower pace than the national benchmark would have required.
sections.whyItMatters: Canada is positioning itself as a reliable alternative supplier to energy-hungry Asian markets. A Pacific outlet would reduce dependence on the US market and capitalize on supply disruptions in the Middle East.

sections.heading: Kazakhstan-Türkiye Deepen Middle Corridor Cooperation
sections.body:
Presidents Tokayev and Erdoğan signed a Declaration on Eternal Friendship and Expanded Strategic Partnership during Erdoğan's visit to Astana, pledging to raise bilateral trade to $15 billion and expand the Trans-Caspian International Transport Route (TITR) — the Middle Corridor.

The corridor, connecting China to Europe via Kazakhstan, Azerbaijan, Georgia and Türkiye, has gained strategic importance as Hormuz disruptions highlight the vulnerability of Middle Eastern routes. Cargo volumes through the corridor reached 2.8 million tons in the first eight months of 2025, with a long-term target of 10 million tons.

Türkiye also signaled interest in moving more Kazakh oil to international markets through its territory, while Kazakhstan invited Turkish companies to expand cargo capacity at Caspian ports Aktau and Kuryk.
sections.whyItMatters: The Middle Corridor is emerging as a strategic alternative route for energy and trade between Asia and Europe, reducing dependence on volatile Middle Eastern chokepoints.

sections.heading: Trump Signals Flexibility on Iran Nuclear Duration
sections.body:
President Trump said a 20-year suspension of Iran's nuclear program would be sufficient for a deal, marking a shift from previous demands for permanent cessation. Speaking after his Beijing summit with Xi Jinping, Trump emphasized it must be a "real 20 years" but did not elaborate on verification mechanisms.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said a lack of trust remains the biggest obstacle in negotiations, expressing openness to diplomatic help from China. Enriched uranium discussions have hit a deadlock, with the IAEA estimating Iran possesses about 440kg of uranium enriched to 60% — theoretically enough for 10-11 nuclear devices.

CENTCOM reports that 78 commercial vessels have been redirected and 4 disabled to ensure compliance with the Hormuz closure. Neither side appears close to a breakthrough, with both rejecting each other's recent proposals.
sections.whyItMatters: A 20-year framework opens space for negotiation but verification remains contentious. The longer the stalemate persists, the more supply tightness will pressure global markets.

sections.heading: Türkiye Energy Angle: Middle Corridor Gains Strategic Weight
sections.body:
Türkiye's strategic position as the western terminus of the Middle Corridor has gained importance as the Hormuz crisis disrupts traditional energy routes. The partnership with Kazakhstan positions both countries as alternative transit hubs for energy and trade between Asia and Europe.

With rail freight between the two countries up 33.9% to 5.9 million tons in the first 11 months of 2025, and road freight at 740,600 tons, the infrastructure is already expanding. Turkish interest in moving more Kazakh oil through its territory aligns with broader efforts to diversify away from Middle Eastern chokepoints.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye stands to benefit from supply chain diversification away from Hormuz-dependent routes, enhancing its role as an energy transit hub connecting Central Asian producers to European markets.

bottomLine: The Hormuz crisis has caused the largest oil supply disruption in history, with OPEC production down 30% and over 1 billion barrels lost. Gulf states with bypass infrastructure are pivoting fast — the UAE accelerating pipeline expansion, Saudi Arabia maxing out the East-West pipeline. Canada is moving to build its first Pacific outlet, while Kazakhstan-Türkiye cooperation on the Middle Corridor reflects a broader reconfiguration of energy trade routes. Trump's acceptance of a 20-year nuclear freeze opens diplomatic space, but with 78 ships redirected and no breakthrough in sight, markets face a long, volatile summer. The crisis is accelerating structural shifts: biofuel alternatives, pipeline investments, and corridor diversification that will reshape energy geopolitics regardless of when Hormuz reopens.
sources: CNBC, Fox Business, BBC, Anadolu Agency, Reuters, IEA, OPEC, Astana Times, CBC, Financial Times
